repos.brief:
**What it does:** An AI agent skill that queries Reddit, X, YouTube, TikTok, HN, Polymarket, and the web in parallel, scores results by real engagement signals (upvotes, likes, prediction market odds), and synthesizes a grounded summary of any topic from the last 30 days.

**Why now:** It hit #1 on GitHub Trending this week with nearly 5,000 stars in seven days, landing as the ecosystem around agent skill marketplaces—Claude Code's plugin system, AgentSkills.io, and tools like OpenClaw—reaches critical mass for composable research workflows.

**Build with it:** Drop it into a sales intelligence agent to auto-brief reps on prospects before calls, or wire it into a trend-detection pipeline that fires when engagement signals across platforms cross a threshold you define.

**What it does:** Taste-Skill is a collection of portable agent skills (markdown files) that inject design taste—layout, typography, motion, spacing—into AI coding agents like Codex, Cursor, and Claude Code to prevent them from generating generic-looking UIs.

**Why now:** It gained 3,023 stars this week, signaling strong developer frustration with the boilerplate aesthetics that current vibe-coding tools produce by default.

**Build with it:** Drop a SKILL.md into your Cursor or Claude Code project, then prompt it to build a frontend—your agent will produce tighter visual hierarchy and less cookie-cutter component styling without manual design intervention.

**What it does:** Headroom is a context compression middleware that sits between your AI agent and the LLM, shrinking tool outputs, logs, RAG chunks, and conversation history by 60–95% using six algorithms, available as a Python/TypeScript library, drop-in proxy, or MCP server.

**Why now:** It hit 18,000+ stars with 2,457 added this week, likely driven by developers hitting context limits and cost walls as they scale agentic pipelines built on Claude, Codex, and Cursor.

**Build with it:** Drop `headroom proxy --port 8787` in front of any existing OpenAI-compatible agent to instantly cut token costs without changing a line of app code, or use the MCP server to add shared, deduplicated memory across multiple agents in the same workflow.

**What it does:** Goose is an open-source, local AI agent built in Rust that can install, execute, edit, and test code across 15+ LLM providers, accessible via desktop app, CLI, or API with MCP extension support.

**Why now:** The project just migrated to the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) and gained nearly 1,000 stars this week, signaling a governance maturation that makes it safer to build production workflows on top of.

**Build with it:** Wire goose into your CI pipeline or internal tooling using its API, connect custom MCP extensions to automate domain-specific tasks, or package a branded distribution with preconfigured providers for your team using the Custom Distros feature.

**What it does:** Agent Reach is a Python CLI that gives AI agents free, zero-config access to Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, GitHub, Bilibili, XiaoHongShu, and a dozen other platforms by wrapping scrapers and open APIs into a single installable tool. It handles authentication, cookie management, and HTML-to-readable-text conversion so agents get clean, structured output instead of raw markup.

**Why now:** The project gained 1,576 stars this week, signaling rapid adoption at the moment MCP (Model Context Protocol) tooling is becoming a standard pattern for extending agents in Claude Code, Cursor, and similar environments. Builders integrating internet-aware agents right now can use it to skip weeks of per-platform scraper work.

**Build with it:** Wire Agent Reach into a research or monitoring agent that pulls Twitter sentiment, Reddit threads, and YouTube transcripts on a topic simultaneously—no paid APIs required. A concrete starting point: an agent that tracks a GitHub repo's issues, correlates them with Reddit complaints, and surfaces a daily digest via RSS.

**What it does:** HyperFrames is an open-source TypeScript framework that renders HTML, CSS, and GSAP animations into deterministic MP4 videos using Puppeteer and FFmpeg, with a CLI and MCP-compatible skills for AI coding agents.

**Why now:** It gained 663 stars this week, signaling strong traction as agent-driven content pipelines become a real production pattern and teams look for programmatic alternatives to timeline-based video editors.

**Build with it:** Drop in your brand tokens via `frame.md`, wire up a Claude Code or Cursor agent with `npx skills add heygen-com/hyperframes`, and generate automated product launch videos, animated data visualizations, or docs-to-video pipelines without touching a video editor.

**What it does:** RTK is a Rust CLI proxy that intercepts commands like `git diff`, `cargo test`, and `grep` before their output reaches an LLM context window, stripping noise to cut token consumption by 60–90% with under 10ms overhead.

**Why now:** It gained 630 stars this week, signaling rapid adoption as Claude Code and similar agentic coding tools become daily drivers and developers feel the direct cost of bloated context in API bills.

**Build with it:** Drop RTK into any agentic coding pipeline or Claude Code workflow to measurably reduce per-session API costs—or extend its 100+ command handlers to add custom output filters for domain-specific tools like database CLIs or Kubernetes commands.

**What it does:** Sub2API is a Go-based API gateway that pools Claude, OpenAI, Gemini, and Antigravity subscriptions behind a single endpoint, handling auth, billing, load balancing, and concurrency control so multiple users can share upstream quotas.

**Why now:** It gained 614 GitHub stars this week, signaling a surge of developer interest likely tied to the rising cost of Claude and Codex subscriptions and the need to split those costs across teams or products.

**Build with it:** Deploy it with Docker, plug in your team's Claude Max or Gemini subscriptions, and spin up per-user API keys with token-level billing — effectively running your own internal AI API reseller or cost-sharing hub without writing billing infrastructure from scratch.

**What it does:** RuView uses ESP32 hardware to capture WiFi Channel State Information and convert radio signal disturbances into real-time presence detection, vital sign monitoring (breathing and heart rate), fall detection, and sleep staging — no cameras, no wearables, no cloud required. It ships with native Home Assistant, Apple Home, Google Home, and Alexa integrations out of the box.

**Why now:** The project gained 601 stars this week, signaling fresh community momentum, and its Matter bridge support lands exactly as the smart home protocol reaches mainstream hardware adoption. The 21-entity-per-node model and three prebuilt HA Blueprints mean builders can wire it into existing automations today without writing integration glue.

**Build with it:** Drop a $9 ESP32 node into a room and pipe CSI data into the Rust edge stack to build a hands-free elderly-care monitor that pages when breathing patterns stop or a bed-exit event fires at 3am. Extend the semantic state layer — which already ships labels like `possible-distress` and `fall-risk-elevated` — with your own spiking neural net classifiers trained on local RF fingerprints in under 30 seconds.

**What it does:** Caveman is a Claude Code skill that instructs AI agents to respond in stripped-down, telegraphic language, cutting output tokens by ~75% while preserving technical accuracy across Claude, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, and 30+ other tools.

**Why now:** It hit 70,000+ stars this week with 572 new ones, signaling a wave of developers actively hunting ways to reduce API costs as token bills scale with heavier agentic workloads.

**Build with it:** Wire caveman into a high-volume coding agent or CI pipeline where Claude explains failures and suggests fixes — same diagnostic quality, fraction of the token cost, faster iteration loops.
